steps.text: Eliminate or reduce hazards.
Implement appropriate engineering and/or administrative controls to address residual risks.
Identify personal protective equipment (PPE) to protect the worker.
Assess the risk of mucous membrane exposure if having contact with NHPs.
Wear protective equipment (e.g., face shield, surgical mask, goggles), as appropriate.
Consider additional PPE for persons working with large animals.
Consider respiratory protection for staff enrolled in a properly constituted respiratory protection program based on the pathogen and work performed.
Fill sink traps with water and/or appropriate disinfectant to prevent the migration of vermin and gases.
Fill open floor drain traps with water and/or appropriate disinfectant or seal them to prevent the migration of vermin and gases.
Submit samples from uninfected source(s) as negative controls, positive control samples, whenever possible.
Blind the testing facility to sources and timing of sample collection.
Caution the exposed staff member that the clinical utility of such assays is not the same as licensed tests and must be interpreted with extreme caution.

Work with the principal investigator, veterinarian, or clinician responsible for the source material to determine if testing appropriate samples could help establish if a specific infectious agent was present.
Apply Lugol’s iodine (1% povidine iodine; 10,000 ppm) to kill N. americanus and S. stercoralis infective larvae on exposed skin.
Disinfect contaminated laboratory surfaces and equipment using 70% ethanol.
Thoroughly clean contaminated surfaces and equipment to remove sticky ascarid and E. vermicularis eggs.
Deactivate ascarid eggs by applying heat at or above 60ºC for more than 15 minutes.
Use BSL-2 practices and facilities for all activities involving the use or manipulation of tissues, cells, blood, or serum from macaques.
Consult guidelines for safely working with macaques.
Handle macaques with strict barrier precaution protocols.
Tend injuries immediately according to the recommendations of the B Virus Working Group led by NIH and CDC.
Transfer or import SE domestically above the permissible amount
Export this agent to another country
Determine what hazards may exist and the risks associated with those hazards.
Monitor current disease outbreaks and compile lists of commonly encountered pathogens for a population, region, or specimen type.
Encourage clinicians to notify the laboratory when they suspect a patient(s) may have an infectious disease that could pose risks to the laboratory professional.

preconditions.text: If necessary to address residual risks
If open floor drains are provided
A staff member is to be screened with a non-commercial assay based on expert consensus
Importation of this agent requires CDC and/or USDA importation permits.
A DoC permit may be required for the export of this agent to another country.
Obtain information regarding exact containment/permit requirements before initiating work.
Prior approval from FSAP must be obtained
A DoC permit may be required

constraints.text: The risk management process must be collaborative and inclusive of all stakeholders.
Persons having contact with NHPs wear protective equipment (e.g., face shield, surgical mask, goggles), as appropriate.
Respiratory protection may be considered for staff enrolled in a properly constituted respiratory protection program.
External facility doors are self-closing and self-locking.
Access to the animal facility is restricted.
Doors to areas where infectious materials and/or animals are housed open inward, are self-closing, are kept closed when experimental animals are present, and are never to be propped open.
Emergency eyewash and shower are readily available, easily accessible, and appropriately maintained.
Negative results may not indicate the absence of a specific infectious agent and should be interpreted with caution.
The administration of an adult diphtheria-tetanus toxoid at ten-year intervals may further reduce the risk of illness to laboratory and animal care personnel.
PPE should be used to cover exposed skin when working with cultures or fecal specimens that may contain infective hookworm or Strongyloides spp. larvae.
Ingestion of infective eggs or transdermal penetration by infective larvae are the primary hazards to laboratory staff and animal care personnel.
Precautions are warranted even when working with formalin-fixed stool specimens as ascarid eggs may continue to develop to the infective stage in formalin.
Ascarid and E. vermicularis eggs are sticky, requiring special care to ensure thorough cleaning.
Consider all macaques potentially infected, regardless of origin or antibody status.
Use gloves, masks, and protective coats, gowns, aprons, or overalls when working with non-human primates or entering animal rooms.
Use a barrier (such as goggles or glasses with solid side shields and masks, or wrap-around face shields) to prevent droplet splashes to eyes, mouth, and nasal passages.
Balance protective equipment specifications with the work to be performed so that barriers do not obscure vision or increase risk of bites, needlesticks, scratches, or splashes.
Caution should be exercised when handling specimens that could potentially contain SARS or MERS-CoV.
Garissa virus is considered an isolate of this virus, so same containment requirements apply.
